step_by_step_thinking:
The key point is identifying where the special visceral afferent (SVA) fibers of the facial nerve terminate. According to article-20094_8 and article-36264_8, the SVA fibers (taste from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue) travel via the nervus intermedius and **terminate in the nucleus of the tractus solitarius** — specifically described as “synapsing” in the nucleus of the solitary tract. Multiple references confirm that taste fibers from the facial nerve (via chorda tympani) project to the *nucleus of the tractus solitarius* (NTS).
None of the listed options mention the nucleus of the tractus solitarius directly — wait! Option C is precisely labeled as "**Nucleus of tractus solitarius**".
Therefore, despite some confusion due to overlapping terminology, the correct anatomical destination for the special visceral afferent fibers of the facial nerve is indeed the **nucleus of tractus solitarius**, confirmed across multiple sources citing synaptic termination in this structure.
\boxed{C} Based on the provided evidence, special visceral afferent fibers from the facial nerve terminate in the nucleus of the tractus solitarius, which integrates taste information from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue received via the chorda tympani. Thus, option C is correct.

According to article-20094_8 and article-36264_8, the SVA fibers (taste from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue) travel via the nervus intermedius and **terminate in the nucleus of the tractus solitarius** — specifically described as “synapsing” in the nucleus of the solitary tract. Multiple references confirm that taste fibers from the facial nerve (via chorda tympani) project to the *nucleus of the tractus solitarius* (NTS).
None of the listed options mention the nucleus of the tractus solitarius directly — wait! Option C is precisely labeled as "**Nucleus of tractus solitarius**".
Therefore, despite some confusion due to overlapping terminology, the correct anatomical destination for the special visceral afferent fibers of the facial nerve is indeed the **nucleus of tractus solitarius**, confirmed across multiple sources citing synaptic termination in this structure.
\boxed{C} Based on the provided evidence, special visceral afferent fibers from the facial nerve terminate in the nucleus of the tractus solitarius, which integrates taste information from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue received via the chorda tympani. Thus, option C is correct.
